Add Buttons</strong></p><ol><li>Create predefined text buttons.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Buttons</strong> toolbar, expand the <strong>OK Button</strong> group.</li><li>Right-click the Back button to expand the text buttons group. Create <em>Back</em>,<em> Next, </em>and<em> Cancel</em> buttons. Guide yourself according to the figure below.</li></ol></li><li>Create regular buttons.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Buttons</strong> toolbar, drag and drop the <strong>Button</strong> symbol onto the diagram pane inside the Frame, next to the <em>Select Template</em> Group Box.</li><li>Select the created button in the diagram pane and type in <em>New.</em></li><li>Create the remaining buttons by repeating steps 2.a and 2.b. Refer to the figure below.</li></ol></li><li>Double-click on the other buttons to invoke their specification window, excluding the <em>Next &gt;</em><span>,</span><em> Cancel</em><span>,</span><em> New,</em><span> and </span><em>Import </em>buttons. Clear the check box <strong>Inactive</strong> to <em>true.</em></li><li>It should look like the figure shown below.</li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="367" ac:width="465"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav59d45e5ced5eaf751d8a4cbe2abf8bbd.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" />Sample of the Report Wizard window</h6><p><strong>Step #6. Using the Report Wizard window</strong></p><p>This User Interface model can now be exported as an image. The steps to do this are as follows:</p><ol><li>Select <strong>Save As Image</strong> from the <strong>File</strong> menu.</li><li>In the Save As Image dialog, select <strong>Active Diagram</strong>.</li><li>In <strong>Image File</strong> define the location where the image should be placed.</li><li>Select <em>Joint Photographic Experts Group (.jpg)</em> (or any format you would like) in <strong>Image Format</strong> and then click Save.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="90fee36a-0a2e-4da5-9773-55a3c3df239c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">982345345897</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb43bef2-6b43-4a53-97ba-d5789e0dafe6"><ac:parameter ac:name="">CaseStudy2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Case Study 2 - Slider Example</h3><p>This case study contains step-by-step instructions for creating a User Interface model with Sliders. It also shows how to customize the symbol properties of User Interface components. However, it does <strong>not</strong> explain how to create a new project and a new diagram. Please refer to &quot;<ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy1"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 1 - Modeling user interface for the Report Wizard dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ac:link>&quot; for those instructions. <span> If you are already familiar with these case studies, please review</span><span> </span><ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy3"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 3 - User Interface Prototyping Example]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ac:link></p><p><strong>Step #1 Create a Frame, Labels and Sliders</strong></p><ol><li>Create a new <strong>Frame</strong>.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Containers</strong> toolbar, click the <strong>Frame</strong> button.</li><li>Drop it on the diagram pane.</li><li>Click on your newly created frame and title <em>Symbol Properties Customization.</em></li></ol></li><li>Add a new <strong>Label</strong> to the Frame.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Tools</strong> toolbar, click the <strong>Label</strong> button. Place the label onto the frame.</li><li>Click the label and change the name to <em>Fill Color.</em></li><li>Create the remaining two labels and name them <em>Text Color</em> and <em>Font Name</em>.</li></ol></li><li>Add <strong>Sliders</strong> to the Frame.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Other</strong> toolbar, click the <strong>Slider</strong> button. Place the slider onto the frame.</li><li>Double-click on the Slider to invoke its specification window.</li><li>In the opened window, set the <strong>Spacing</strong> property value to 50.</li><li>Set the <strong>Knob Position</strong> property value to 0 in order to move the knob to the left position.</li><li>In the Slider's specification window, select the <strong>Values</strong> property and click the plus sign button. A Values dialog window opens. If there are values already defined in the window, click the minus sign button to delete them. </li><li>To add new values, click the plus sign button and then type <em>0 Red</em>. <em>0 Red</em> must be written in a specific way. First, type in <em>0</em> and press <strong>Enter</strong>. Type <em>Red </em>on the next line. Rename the other two given values to <em>50 Blue</em> and <em>100 White</em> using the same procedure.</li><li>Draw two more sliders and repeat steps 3a - 3f until your frame looks like the one shown in the figure below.</li></ol></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8625b9f4-ffb5-4447-9e18-e673dc79fc66"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Notation Formality</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When setting the values of a slider it is important to note that there is no empty space between <em>0</em>. For example, <em>0</em> represents a new line. When entering a value, it should look like this:</p><p><ac:image ac:align="left" ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2017-07-22 at 1.03.50 PM.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="256" ac:width="501"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav53d3c9355ce1fa8f8aeb943c85e060d5.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> Symbol Properties Customization Frame with Added Labels and Sliders</h6><p><strong>Step #2 Add Text Fields</strong></p><ol><li>Create Text Fields.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Text</strong> toolbar. click the <strong>Text Field</strong> button. Drop it onto the frame. </li><li>Name the text field <em>Red Background</em>.</li><li>Repeat the previous two steps for the remaining two text fields, as shown in the figure below.</li></ol></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="255" ac:width="501"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0212b54c35d95fb5cb12d97d73b2c6a3.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Added Text Fields to the Frame</h6><p><strong>Step #3 Edit Symbol Properties for the Text Fields</strong></p><ol><li>Edit <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> for the first text field.<ol><li>Select the first text field on the Frame shape.</li><li>Right-click it and select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> from its shortcut menu.</li><li>Set the <strong>Use Fill Color</strong> property to true and change the <strong>Fill Color</strong> property value to red. Change the fill color by clicking the ... to the right of the field, and in the Color dialog, click <strong>RGB </strong>and type in <em>FF0000</em> in the <strong>Color Code</strong> field.</li></ol></li><li>Edit <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> for the remaining fields.<ol><li>Follow steps 1a - 1b for the next steps.</li><li>For the second text field, select yellow in <strong>Text Color</strong>.</li><li>For the third one, select the font name Tahoma in <strong>Font</strong>, as shown in the figure below </li></ol></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="257" ac:width="500"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav4a6ff3bdd67c8337921149a2d11507f0.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Finalized Slider Example</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="83028fde-0aa2-472a-aac9-b0d6acc6c427"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">458973459054</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="223a7361-ed93-4ecd-b934-a2513a86262c"><ac:parameter ac:name="">CaseStudy3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Case Study 3 - User Interface Prototyping Example</h3><p>This case study demonstrates how to connect several user interface models and create a browsable report. The models should represent a test application with a Login Dialog, a Test Browser and a test with several questions. Here are step-by-step instructions on how to add hyperlinks and create browsable reports. <span>If you are already familiar with this case study, please review </span><ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy2"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 2 - Slider Example]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ac:link><span>, or <ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy1"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 1 - Modeling user interface for the Report Wizard dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ac:link></span></p><p><strong>Step #1 Create the first Model</strong></p><ol><li><p><span>Create a new Package for the model.</span></p></li><li><p><span>Create a model similar to the one shown below.</span></p></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="179" ac:width="318"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0c437b6b5c5cf1161007d0bd842f87e7.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">First Model of Prototyping Example - Login Dialog</span></h6><p><strong>Step #2 Create the second Model</strong></p><ol><li><p>Create another new Package for this model.</p></li><li><p>Create a model similar to the one depicted below.</p></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="238" ac:width="488"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavc6cb8ac9da4f9b8f2fe40099cf5f9e86.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Second Model of Prototyping Example - Test Browser</h6><p><strong>Step #3 Create remaining Models</strong></p><ol><li><p>Create a separate Package for each model.</p></li><li><p>You can build models similar to the ones shown in the detailed samples mentioned in the beginning of this chapter, or create your own in a similar manner.</p></li></ol><p><strong>Step #4 Add Hyperlinks</strong></p><ol><li><p>Add a hyperlink.</p><ol><li><p>Open the model with the Login Dialog.</p></li><li><p>Open the Specification window of the <strong>OK</strong> button.</p></li><li>Next to the Active Hyperlink property field, click the ... button. The <strong>Edit Hyperlink</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>In the Edit Hyperlink dialog, select <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Element/Symbol</strong> and click the &quot;...&quot; button. Browse to the Package <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Test Browser</strong>, select the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="User Interface Modeling diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[User Interface diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in it and confirm two times with OK - a diagram symbol will appear next to the OK button. Double-clicking the OK button will lead to the other diagram.</li></ol></li><li><p>Now connect all the buttons in the other diagrams with hyperlinks. The diagram symbol appears next to a component with a hyperlink.</p></li></ol><p><strong>Step #5 Create a browsable Report</strong></p><ol><li><p>Create a new report.</p><ol><li><p>Select <strong>Report Wizard</strong> from the <strong>Tools</strong> menu.</p></li><li><p>Expand the <strong>Default Template</strong> in the tree and select <strong>Web Publisher 2.0</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Confirm three times by clicking <strong>Next</strong> until the dialog allows you to add data to the report. Add all Packages that contain the created diagrams and click <strong>Next</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Give the <strong>Report file</strong> a name to the output file. Clear the check box <strong>Display in viewer after generating report</strong>. </p></li><li><p>Click <strong>Generate</strong>. The report is built and shown in your default browser.</p></li></ol></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="237797ab-41a2-406e-b300-60af06507ca6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">433458345987534</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related References:</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML diagrams" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Extension diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Extension diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714966 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00166: Case study #1: Adding a rule to create a Generalization relationship on drag-and-drop

- page_id: `136714966`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714966/Case+study+1+Adding+a+rule+to+create+a+Generalization+relationship+on+drag-and-drop
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:58.746+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data > Customizing Drag-and-Drop
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: New Button

1254568729

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: New Button

1254568740

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: New Button

1254568739

This example presents step-by-step instructions for customizing a drag-and-drop rule, according to the Generalization relationship created between classes after the drag-and-drop.

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='MD2024x'] .
2. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024x'] , and name it Class onto Class .
3. For the *Class onto Class* class, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024x']apply the «*DragAndDropSpecification*» stereotype. New ButtonThere is a new button in the Profile diagram palette - Drag and Drop Specification. Use this button to create a class element with the assigned «*DragAndDropSpecification*» stereotype.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Elements' space='MD2024x']Create a *Customization* element. 
In the **Customization** Specification window, assign the *Class onto Class* element to the **Allowed Drag and Drops** property under **Connection Rules**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. In the**Customization**Specification window, under the**General**category, for the**Customization Target**property, assign the*Class*metaclass. [ATTACHMENT filename='drag_and_drop2.png']
6. In the*Class onto Class*Specification window, specify the following property values:For the **Source Element** property, specify the *Class* metaclass.For the **Relation Action Result** property, specify the *Generalization* metaclass.For the**Representation Text** property, specify the name of the rule, for example, *Create Generalization*.
7. Reopen the project. Drag the class to the other class, the Generalization relationship will be created between these classes. [ATTACHMENT filename='drag_and_drop7.png']

1254568728

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714970 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00167: Case study #2: Adding a rule to assign a property type on drag-and-drop

- page_id: `136714970`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714970/Case+study+2+Adding+a+rule+to+assign+a+property+type+on+drag-and-drop
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:58.858+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data > Customizing Drag-and-Drop
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1254617849

1254617870

1254617859

This example presents step-by-step instructions for customizing a drag-and-drop rule. You will assign the property type after dragging a class to the property.

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='MD2024x'].
2. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024x'], and name it *Classifie*r *on Property*.
3. For the *Classifier on Property* class, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024x']apply the «*DragAndDropSpecification*» stereotype.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Elements' space='MD2024x'].
5. In the **Customization** [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'], assign the *Classifier on Property* element to the **Allowed Drag and Drops** property, .
6. In the **Customization**Specification window, assign the *Property*metaclass to the **Customization Target**property. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. In the *Classifier on Property* Specification window, specify the following property values:
  - For the **Source Element** property, specify the *Classifier* metaclass.
  - For the **Property Action Result** property, specify *Type*.
  - For the **Representation Text**property, type the name of the rule, for example, *Assign Type*. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Reopen the project. The classifier is assigned as a property type after dragging it to the property. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

1254617848

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Numbering Customizations' space='MD2024x']

## PAGE 00168: Cases of advanced editing

- page_id: `136711568`
- space_key: `MD2024x`

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You may have noticed that editing some property values is a sophisticated process. These are the cases of editing such property values:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating inner element as property value' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Assigning value specification as property value' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">You may have noticed that editing some property values is a sophisticated process. These are the cases of <span>editing such property values:</span></span></p><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating inner element as property value" /></ac:link></span></span></span></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Assigning value specification as property value" /></ac:link></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Editing property values in property groups" /></ac:link></span></span></span><br /></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="792878db-a431-4440-b40a-922e3d565ff4"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5773f9ff-ac57-4a8c-aba4-079996a70ab3"><ac:parameter ac:name="all">true</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="depth">1</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Editing property values" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709715 space=MD2024x version=3 -->
## PAGE 00170: CATIA FLXML Exporter

- page_id: `136709715`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709715/CATIA+FLXML+Exporter
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:48:06.267+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > CATIA applications
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Prerequisites

1531195013

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Prerequisites

1531195024

INLINE

524342662

INLINE

**On this page**

4

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Prerequisites

1531195014

INLINE

The CATIA FLXML Exporter plugin allows you to transform a UML/SysML/UAF model to functional and logical structures supported in the **3D**EXPERIENCE****platform. The plugin uses the FLXML Generation Engine and the element transformation schema to generate an FLXM file from the selected model elements. The conversion of CATIA Magic data to **3D**EXPERIENCE****platform data with lifecycle management is based on element mapping.

You can later [import the generated FLXML file to the **3D**EXPERIENCE****platform](https://help.3ds.com/2024x/English/DSDoc/FleUserMap/fle-t-XMLImport.htm?ContextScope=onpremise) while ensuring the digital continuity and master interfaces of system architecture (with sub-systems developed in **3D**EXPERIENCE****Platform, CATIA Magic, or other tools). The CATIA FLXM Exporter plugin also helps to initiate the transition from the conceptual to the design layer.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To be able to export the selected part of a model to an FLXML file, you need to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing modeling tools and plugins' space='IL2024x'] in your modeling tool.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The schema displaying how the CATIA FLXML Exporter plugin works.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Using the CATIA FLXML Exporter plugin ensures the digital continuity and master interfaces of the system architecture.

##### Exporting model data to FLXML

To export model data to an FLXML file, follow the steps described below.

To export model data to an FLXML file

1. In the main menu of a modeling tool, go to 3DEXPERIENCE > CATIA FLXML Exporter > Generate FLXML . The CATIA FLXML Exporter dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='CATIA_FLXML_exporter_dialog.png']
2. In the open dialog, click [ATTACHMENT filename='select_scope.png'] next to the Scope elements box and select the scope of elements to be included in the generated FLXML file: [IMAGE alt='' src=''] By default, all elements owned by the selected scope element are included when generating an FLXML file.If the selected scope element is a diagram, the scope of the FLXML file generation will be the elements displayed in the diagram.If a certain element type is not included in the default element mapping schema, the elements of this type are excluded when generating an FLXML file.
  1. In the Tree view of the Select scope elements dialog, select the elements you want to export. (Press and hold the Ctrl key to select multiple elements.)
  2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='add_scope.png'] to add the elements to the Selected elements list.
  3. Click OK to close the dialog.
3. In the List of available schemas box, select the default SysML to FL schema or a custom schema.
4. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='select_scope.png'] next to the Destination folder box and select the folder where the generated FLXML file should be stored.
5. In the CATIA FLXML Exporter dialog, click OK to start the export.
6. If you get the message saying that some validation errors were found, do one of the following:
  1. Click Yes to ignore the validation errors and generate an FLXML file anyway.
  2. Click No to correct the errors in the open validation results panel. Then try exporting your model to an FLXML file again.

After completing the above steps, an FLXML file is generated and saved in the destination folder. In addition, the **CATIA FLXM Results** window opens, where you can view and filter the export results or fix the validation errors.

##### Exporting FLXML results

Once you , the **CATIA FLXML Results** panel opens, displaying the export results. The results include both transformed and not transformed elements and file generation errors. You can export FLXML results to an MS Excel file as described below.

To export FLXML results to an MS Excel file

1. In the CATIA FLXML Results panel, click [ATTACHMENT filename='export_MSExcel.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='exporting_FLXML_results.png']
2. In the Select Destination Directory dialog, select the folder where you want to save the file with FLXML results and click Save .

The MS Excel file with FLXML results is automatically opened and saved in the specified directory.

1531195012

INLINE

**Additional resources**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='CATIA FLXML Exporter Plugin.mp4']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='CATIA FLXML Exporter Plugin - Effectivity.mp4']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The conversion of CATIA Magic data to <strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE<strong> </strong>platform data with lifecycle management is based on element mapping.</p><p>You can later <a href="https://help.3ds.com/2024x/English/DSDoc/FleUserMap/fle-t-XMLImport.htm?ContextScope=onpremise">import the generated FLXML file to the <strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE<strong> </strong>platform</a> while ensuring the digital continuity and master interfaces of system architecture (with sub-systems developed in <strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE<strong> </strong>Platform, CATIA Magic, or other tools). The CATIA FLXM Exporter plugin also helps to initiate the transition from the conceptual to the design layer.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e9e33596-f72b-4bba-95d4-5b8d1a8a2ba0"><ac:rich-text-body>For detailed information about the CATIA FLXML Exporter plugin, see <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CATIA FLXML Exporter Plugin User Guide.pdf" /></ac:link></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="08ec6ff2-aa94-4b92-935a-679517a15172"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To be able to export the selected part of a model to an FLXML file, you need to <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL2024x" ri:content-title="Installing modeling tools and plugins" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[install the CATIA FLXML Exporter Plugin]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in your modeling tool.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="flxml_exporter_plugin.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The schema displaying how the CATIA FLXML Exporter plugin works.</h6><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="importing_flxml_file.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Using the CATIA FLXML Exporter plugin ensures the digital continuity and master interfaces of the system architecture.</h6><h3><br />Exporting model data to FLXML</h3><p>To export model data to an FLXML file, follow the steps described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To export model data to an FLXML file</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu of a modeling tool, go to <strong>3DEXPERIENCE</strong> &gt; <strong>CATIA FLXML Exporter</strong> &gt; <strong>Generate FLXML</strong>. The <strong>CATIA FLXML Exporter</strong> dialog opens.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="CATIA_FLXML_exporter_dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the open dialog, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="23"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_scope.png" /></ac:image> next to the <strong>Scope elements</strong> box and select the scope of elements to be included in the generated FLXML file:<ol><li>In the <strong>Tree</strong> view of the <strong>Select scope elements</strong> dialog, select the elements you want to export. (Press and hold the <strong>Ctrl</strong> key to select multiple elements.)</li><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_scope.png" /></ac:image> to add the elements to the <strong>Selected elements</strong> list.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the dialog.<br /><br /></li></ol><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="selecting_scope.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a758e3dc-f4aa-44a5-95e9-0ad14e942469"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><p>By default, all elements owned by the selected scope element are included when generating an FLXML file.</p></li><li><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">If the selected scope element is a diagram, the scope of the FLXML file generation will be the elements displayed in the diagram.</span></p></li><li><p>If a certain element type is not included in the default element mapping schema, the elements of this type are excluded when generating an FLXML file.<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><br /></span></p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>In the <strong>List of available schemas</strong> box, select the default <strong>SysML to FL</strong> schema or a custom schema.</li><li>Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="23"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_scope.png" /></ac:image> next to the <strong>Destination folder</strong> box and select the folder where the generated FLXML file should be stored.</li><li>In the <strong>CATIA FLXML Exporter</strong> dialog, click <strong>OK</strong> to start the export.</li><li>If you get the message saying that some validation errors were found, do one of the following:<ol><li>Click <strong>Yes</strong> to ignore the validation errors and generate an FLXML file anyway.</li><li>Click <strong>No</strong> to correct the errors in the open validation results panel. Then try exporting your model to an FLXML file again.</li></ol></li></ol><p><br />After completing the above steps, an FLXML file is generated and saved in the destination folder. In addition, the <strong>CATIA FLXM Results</strong> window opens, where you can view and filter the export results or fix the validation errors.</p><h3><br />Exporting FLXML results</h3><p>Once you <ac:link ac:anchor="Exporting model data to FLXML"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[export model data to an FLXML file]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, the <strong>CATIA FLXML Results</strong> panel opens, displaying the export results. The results include both transformed and not transformed elements and file generation errors. You can export FLXML results to an MS Excel file as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To export FLXML results to an MS Excel file</p><hr /><ol><li>In the <strong>CATIA FLXML Results</strong> panel, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="export_MSExcel.png" /></ac:image>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="exporting_FLXML_results.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the <strong>Select Destination Directory</strong> dialog, select the folder where you want to save the file with FLXML results and click <strong>Save</strong>.</li></ol><p><br />The MS Excel file with FLXML results is automatically opened and saved in the specified directory. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="284f9529-f4f8-40f2-9ba2-67925dbb2847"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1531195012</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Additional resources</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CATIA FLXML Exporter Plugin.mp4" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CATIA FLXML Exporter Plugin - Effectivity.mp4" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=160760641 space=MD2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00171: CATIA Software Producer

- page_id: `160760641`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/160760641/CATIA+Software+Producer
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2024-03-06T11:01:58.315+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > CATIA applications
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page:**

**4**

The CATIA Software Producer allows you to generate and build code from a UML or SysML model. You can generate code, for example, from the UML Class structure, UML State Machine, or SysML Blocks. The code generation and building are executed via the **3D**EXPERIENCE platform.

##### **Key concepts and capabilities**

CATIA Software Producer offers the following generation capabilities:

- Generating C++ classes from UML structured classes
- Generating C and C++ code from UML behaviors
- Generating target dependent middleware code including AUTOSAR classic and adaptive
- Generating and compiling code for FMU
- Generating code from a model persisted as a file, or in TeamWorkCloud or in the 3DEXPERIENCE Platform.
- Generate code containing traceability information establishing the relation from the model items to the code.

For more information about the Software Production Engineering application, see [Software Production Engineering](https://help.3ds.com/2023x/english/dsdoc/CatEspUserMap/catesp-c-ov.htm?contextscope=cloud&id=bb196d4c58f14192a3a2c4c1fbefb4c6).

CATIA Software Producer uses the concept of target configuration to guide the user in configuring the code generation process.

##### Prerequisites

- The CATIA Software Producer Client plugin is installed in your modeling tool. Learn more about how to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Downloading installation files' space='IL2022xR2'] .
- The **3D**EXPERIENCE platform is on the cloud.
- You have the Software Production Engineer Role. It will give you permission to use the service.

Before starting to work on code generation, first, you have to **connect to 3DEXPERIENCE** by entering the URL of the Software Producer cloud server. After that, you will be able to log in with your regular identifiers.

For information about how to authenticate with the **3D**EXPERIENCE platform, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Authentication with 3DEXPERIENCE platform' space='']Authentication with **3D**EXPERIENCE platform.

If you start working with CATIA Software Producer for the first time in your project, a dialog will open to configure the local workspace path which will host generated resources.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Configuring the code generator

###### The principle

The generated code is composed of a behavior part and a middleware part. It is identified as a target. The behavior part is relative to the behavior of your application (such as State Machine) whereas the middleware part is target-specific and is generated from the architecture of your application (such as IBD).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As the code is composed of multiple entities, multiple configurations are required. You will be guided in the configuration through multiple dialogs to create the target configuration.

code_generationTo configure the code generator

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the open SysML diagram:
    1. Select the element you want to generate. If you do not select any particular element in the diagram, the entire diagram view is selected as the generation input.
    2. In the diagram toolbar, click the Configure Target [ATTACHMENT filename='configure_target.png'] button.
  - In the Containment tree:
    1. Select the element you want to generate.
    2. From the selected element's shortcut menu, select Software Code Generation and click Configure Target . [ATTACHMENT filename='run_configure_target.png']
2. In the open **Generator Details** dialog, configure the target of your generation:
  1. Select the operation in charge of the target you expect. For example, the target can be C code, C++ code or an FMU. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
  2. (optional) Check and specify the download location if you want the results saved on your local file system. The generated files will be downloaded under this location, in a folder named after the configuration name.
3. Click the OK button.

If the operation you selected (for example FMU Target Build) requires additional operations to be executed, additional dialogs open to configure them.

Once the end of the configuration process is reached, i.e. you have fully configured your target, under the Configuration package you will find:

- The Generator Configurations package 1 : this is where classes are created to save common properties of generators (parameters especially).
- The Target Configurations package 1 : this is where packages are created to save the flow of a target. Within this package, classes are created to save specific properties for each SPO (download options, input, and generator mapping).

1You can change the default name of those packages in the **Environment Options** > **CATIA Software Producer Client General Properties** > **Configuration section**

The configurations are saved in the model via the “CATIA – Embedded Software Profile” (*EmbeddedSoftwareProfile.mdzip*) profile.

##### Executing the code generator

To execute the code generator

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the open SysML diagram:
    1. Select the element you want to generate. If you do not select any particular element in the diagram, the entire diagram view is selected as the generation input.
    2. In the diagram toolbar, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='code_generation.png'] Generate Target button and choose Generate Target . [ATTACHMENT filename='generate_target.png']
  - In the Containment tree:
    1. Select the element you want to generate.
    2. From the selected element's shortcut menu, select Software Code Generation and click Generate Target .

The target will be generated based on your configuration:

- If you click Generate Target , the generation will be performed for the modified elements in the model.
- If you click Full Generate Target , the generation will be fully performed.

##### Edit or change your configuration

To edit generator configuration properties

1. In the Containment tree, right-click a Class element of the needed generator configuration.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Software Code Generation and select Edit Configuration .
3. In the open Generation Configuration dialog, make your changes.
4. Click Apply and then Close .

[IMAGE alt='' src=''][IMAGE alt='' src='']

If you have configured multiple targets for the same input, you might want to change the default one (which was created last).

To change the default target configuration

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the diagram toolbar, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='change_default_target.png'] Change Default Target button.
  - In the selected element's shortcut menu, click Software Code Generation > Change Default Target .
2. Select the target you want to use. [ATTACHMENT filename='choose_default_target.png']
3. Click OK .

##### Local workspace of the software producer

Under the local workspace, you can find the generated files, if you have selected the download option and left the default location (as seen above) in the configurations. You can also find some other files under an “etc” folder:

- <Local_workspace_path>
  - <componentA_name>
    - <configuration1_name>
    - <configuration2_name>
  - <componentB_name>
    - <configuration3_name>
    - <configuration4_name>
  - etc
    - operations
      - json 3
    - backup 2
    - logs
      - log 4

1You can change the root of the local workspace path in the project options.

2By default, a backup of your downloaded files is saved in this folder so that in case something wrong happens with the download of new files, you can still reach your previous files. You can disable this option in the **Project Options**

3SPO Report files are rotated by size and date so that you might see multiple report files here.

4Log files are rotated by size and date so that you might see multiple log files here.

##### RoundTrip

The **roundtrip** action is a feature available for State Machines and Opaque Behaviors. Using the roundtrip action, you can add the modification you made in the code in between BEG_USER_CODE / END_USER_CODE tags (in files commonly named <ComponentName>_usr.c), back into the model and synchronize the file on the workspace on the cloud.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Views

###### Software Producer Logs

In the Software Producer Log panel, you can see the messages of the running SPO.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

This information is saved in the log file next to your project files. To open the log file location quickly, in the **Software Producer Logs** window, click the **Show Log** **File** button. Each project has its own log file.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

A log file for general Software Producer messages not related to a specific project is stored next to the modeling tool log file.

At the bottom right corner of the modeling tool, in the progress bar, you can see whether the generation is running. After the generation is completed, the notification is displayed in case of failure or always if the verbose mode is activated (**Environment Options**> **CATIA Software Producer Client General Properties** > **Notifications** section).

###### Validation Results

When an operation produces a report, the plugin reads it and opens it in a **Validation Result** window if any element has a diagnostic associated.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

A diagnostic is associated with a rule consisting of a severity, message, and source.

You can navigate to the source in the Containment tree. If you need to show more details concerning the rule or open the full report, right-click the appropriate lines of the table.

You can turn off the **Validation Results** window's opening after the operation produces a report.

To turn the validation report off

1. In the modeling tool main menu, click Options and select Project .
2. Set the **Show Validation Report**property to *false*.

###### Diagrams and Containment tree

The following commands are available from the diagrams toolbar or from the shortcut menu of the supported elements in the Containment tree:

| Button | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Configure Target | Configure the code generators according to the first operation you selected (this first operation will be your final target). You can manage the parameters of the generators here, as well as the download options. |
| Change Default Target | You can change the target selected for the current element. |
| Code Generation | Generates the code for the selected elements in, for example, the State Machine or Internal Block Definition diagrams. If the entire diagram is selected, the code is generated for the whole content.The generation result is downloaded to your machine if you select the download option.By default, the classic generation is triggered, but you can select the full generation to generate code in all circumstances. |
| Show in File Explorer | Open a location in your file system wherein the Software Production Operation results file is stored. |
| Roundtrip | The changes made in the generated code are applied in the State Machine diagram or Opaque Behavior. |

##### Options

You can **deactivate the Software Production Operation** you do not need.

To deactivate the Software Production Operation

1. In the main menu, click Options > Environment . The Environment Options dialog opens.
2. In the dialog, go to CATIA Software Producer Client > Operations .
3. Select the needed operation and click Change activation status.

You can switch projects (or even shut down your computer, saving your work first) while waiting for the generation result as it is running on the cloud and as the widget keeps the IDs of the cloud assets in the record files located in <local workspace path>\etc\operations\spoRecordFile.json (you will have access to it if you need it, for example, for the debugging purpose). When you come back, another dialog will tell you that operations were running while you were away and will propose you inspect the result and download it.

##### Additional Information

A workspace is automatically created on our cloud servers for each of your projects using the widget. This workspace gathers the assets needed for the generation. A cloud workspace is automatically deleted after 30 days of inactivity. You can also delete it manually in the**Environment Options**dialog. If you delete the workspace, all the components will have to be regenerated on the cloud on your next session because you will not be able to push any files to a workspace through the plugin.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page:</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8f4a8171-85b3-4f0f-80ca-c51231b1d727"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><hr /></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>The CATIA Software Producer allows you to generate and build code from a UML or SysML model. You can generate code, for example, from the UML Class structure,  UML State Machine, or SysML Blocks. The code generation and building are executed via the <strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE platform. </p><h3><strong>Key concepts and capabilities</strong></h3><p>CATIA Software Producer offers the following generation capabilities:</p><ul><li>Generating C++ classes from UML structured classes</li><li>Generating C and C++ code from UML behaviors</li><li>Generating target dependent middleware code including AUTOSAR classic and adaptive</li><li>Generating and compiling code for FMU</li><li>Generating code from a model persisted as a file, or in TeamWorkCloud or in the 3DEXPERIENCE Platform.</li><li>Generate code containing traceability information establishing the relation from the model items to the code.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="74dac8af-7f67-49a3-81d1-4cd204eaddc5"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For more information about the Software Production Engineering application, see <a href="https://help.3ds.com/2023x/english/dsdoc/CatEspUserMap/catesp-c-ov.htm?contextscope=cloud&amp;id=bb196d4c58f14192a3a2c4c1fbefb4c6">Software Production Engineering</a>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>CATIA Software Producer uses the concept of target configuration to guide the user in configuring the code generation process.</p><h3><span>Prerequisites</span></h3><ul><li>The CATIA Software Producer Client plugin is installed in your modeling tool. Learn more about how to <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL2022xR2" ri:content-title="Downloading installation files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[download the installation files]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><p>The <strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE platform is on the cloud.</p></li><li><p><span>You have the Software Production Engineer Role. It will give you permission to use the service.</span></p></li></ul><p>Before starting to work on code generation, first, you have to <strong>connect to 3DEXPERIENCE</strong> by entering the URL of the Software Producer cloud server. After that, you will be able to log in with your regular identifiers.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aff9f90f-d91b-430b-aa86-feb291d9a31b"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For information about how to authenticate with the <strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE platform, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Authentication with 3DEXPERIENCE platform" /><ac:link-body>Authentication with <strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE platform</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If you start working with CATIA Software Producer for the first time in your project, a dialog will open to configure the local workspace path which will host generated resources.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="local_workspace.png" /></ac:image></p><h3>Configuring the code generator</h3><h4>The principle </h4><p>The generated code is composed of a behavior part and a middleware part. It is identified as a target. The behavior part is relative to the behavior of your application (such as State Machine) whereas the middleware part is target-specific and is generated from the architecture of your application (such as IBD).</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="schema.png" /></ac:image></p><p>As the code is composed of multiple entities, multiple configurations are required. You will be guided in the configuration through multiple dialogs to create the target configuration.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a0baa82f-d4a3-4281-b7b3-9fff692446aa"><ac:parameter ac:name="">code_generation</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To configure the code generator</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the open SysML diagram:<ol><li>Select the element you want to generate. If you do not select any particular element in the diagram, the entire diagram view is selected as the generation input.</li><li>In the diagram toolbar, click the Configure Target <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="22"><ri:attachment ri:filename="configure_target.png" /></ac:image>button. </li></ol></li><li>In the Containment tree:<ol><li>Select the element you want to generate.</li><li>From the selected element's shortcut menu, select <strong>Software </strong><strong>Code Generation</strong> and click <strong>Configure Target</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="run_configure_target.png" /></ac:image></li></ol></li></ul></li><li><p><span>In the open <strong>Generator Details</strong> dialog, configure the target of your generation:</span></p><ol><li><p><span>Select the operation in charge of the target you expect. For example, the target can be C code, C++ code or an FMU.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="generator_details.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p></li><li><span>(optional) Check and specify the download location if you want the results saved on your local file system. The generated files will be downloaded under this location, in a folder named after the configuration name.</span></li></ol></li><li>Click the <strong>OK </strong><span>button.</span></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>If the operation you selected (for example FMU Target Build) requires additional operations to be executed, additional dialogs open to configure them.</p><p>Once the end of the configuration process is reached, i.e. you have fully configured your target, <span> under the Configuration package you will find:</span></p><ul><li><strong>The Generator Configurations package</strong><sup>1</sup>: this is where classes are created to save common properties of generators (parameters especially).</li><li><strong>The Target Configurations package</strong><sup>1</sup>: this is where packages are created to save the flow of a target. <span>Within this package,  classes are created to save specific properties for each SPO (download options, input, and generator mapping). </span></li></ul><hr /><p><span><sup>1</sup>You can change the default name of those packages in the <strong>Environment Options</strong> &gt; <strong>CATIA Software Producer Client General Properties</strong> &gt; <strong>Configuration section</strong></span></p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfb6f921-dc42-461d-9a5f-d359c5452be7"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The configurations are saved in the model via the “CATIA – Embedded Software Profile” (<em>EmbeddedSoftwareProfile.mdzip</em>) profile.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Executing the code generator</h3><p>To execute the code generator</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<br /><ul><li>In the open SysML diagram:<ol><li>Select the element you want to generate. If you do not select any particular element in the diagram, the entire diagram view is selected as the generation input.</li><li>In the diagram toolbar, click the <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="24"><ri:attachment ri:filename="code_generation.png" /></ac:image>Generate Target button and choose <strong>Generate Target</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="generate_target.png" /></ac:image></li></ol></li><li>In the Containment tree:<br /><ol><li>Select the element you want to generate. </li><li>From the selected element's shortcut menu, select <strong>Software Code Generation</strong> and click <strong>Generate Target</strong>.</li></ol></li></ul></li></ol><p>The target will be generated based on your configuration:</p><ul><li>If you click <strong>Generate Target</strong>, the generation will be performed for the modified elements in the model.</li><li>If you click <strong>Full Generate Target</strong>, the generation will be fully performed.</li></ul><h3>Edit or change your configuration</h3><p>To edit generator configuration properties</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Containment tree, right-click a Class element of the needed generator configuration.</li><li>In the shortcut menu, click <strong>Software Code Generation</strong> and select <strong>Edit Configuration</strong>.</li><li>In the open <strong>Generation Configuration</strong> dialog, make your changes.</li><li>Click <strong>Apply </strong>and then <strong>Close</strong>.</li></ol><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="edit_configuration_properties.png" /></ac:image><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="edit_configuration_properties_I.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p>If you have configured multiple targets for the same input, you might want to change the default one (which was created last).</p><p>To change the default target configuration</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the diagram toolbar, click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="change_default_target.png" /></ac:image>Change Default Target button.  </li><li>In the selected element's shortcut menu, click <strong>Software Code Generation</strong> &gt; <strong>Change Default Target</strong>. </li></ul></li><li>Select the target you want to use. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="choose_default_target.png" /></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><h3>Local workspace of the software producer</h3><p>Under the local workspace, you can find the generated files, if you have selected the download option and left the default location (as seen above) in the configurations. You can also find some other files under an “etc” folder:</p><ul><li>&lt;Local_workspace_path&gt;<ul><li>&lt;componentA_name&gt;<ul><li>&lt;configuration1_name&gt;</li><li>&lt;configuration2_name&gt;</li></ul></li><li>&lt;componentB_name&gt;<ul><li>&lt;configuration3_name&gt;</li><li>&lt;configuration4_name&gt;</li></ul></li><li>etc<ul><li>operations<ul><li>json<sup>3</sup></li></ul></li><li>backup<sup>2</sup></li><li>logs<ul><li>log<sup>4 </sup></li></ul></li></ul></li></ul></li></ul><hr /><p><sup>1</sup>You can change the root of the local workspace path in the project options.</p><p><sup>2</sup>By default, a backup of your downloaded files is saved in this folder so that in case something wrong happens with the download of new files, you can still reach your previous files. You can disable this option in the <strong>Project Options </strong></p><p><sup>3</sup>SPO Report files are rotated by size and date so that you might see multiple report files here.</p><p><sup>4</sup>Log files are rotated by size and date so that you might see multiple log files here.</p><h3>RoundTrip</h3><p>The <strong>roundtrip</strong> action is a feature available for State Machines and Opaque Behaviors. Using the roundtrip action, you can add the modification you made in the code in between BEG_USER_CODE / END_USER_CODE tags (in files commonly named &lt;ComponentName&gt;_usr.c), back into the model and synchronize the file on the workspace on the cloud.</p><p><ac:image ac:height="140"><ri:attachment ri:filename="roundtrip_example.png" /></ac:image></p><h3>Views</h3><h4>Software Producer Logs</h4><p>In the Software Producer Log panel, you can see the messages of the running SPO.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="log.png" /></ac:image></p><p>This information is saved in the log file next to your project files. To open the log file location quickly, in the <strong>Software Producer Logs</strong> window, click the <strong>Show Log</strong> <strong>File</strong> button. Each project has its own log file.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="show log file.png" /></ac:image></p><p>A log file for general Software Producer messages not related to a specific project is stored next to the modeling tool log file.</p><p>At the bottom right corner of the modeling tool, in the progress bar, you can see whether the generation is running. After the generation is completed, the notification is displayed in case of failure or always if the verbose mode is activated (<span><strong>Environment Options </strong>&gt; <strong>CATIA Software Producer Client General Properties</strong> &gt; <strong>Notifications</strong> section</span>).</p><h4>Validation Results</h4><p>When an operation produces a report, the plugin reads it and opens it in a <strong>Validation Result</strong> window if any element has a diagnostic associated.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="validation_results_window.png" /></ac:image></p><p>A diagnostic is associated with a rule consisting of a severity, message, and source.</p><p>You can navigate to the source in the Containment tree.  If you need to show more details concerning the rule or open the full report, right-click the appropriate lines of the table.</p><p>You can turn off the <strong>Validation Results</strong> window's opening after the operation produces a report.</p><p><br /></p><p>To turn the validation report off</p><hr /><ol><li>In the modeling tool main menu, click <strong>Options </strong>and select <strong>Project</strong>.</li><li><span>Set the <strong>Show Validation Report </strong>property to <em>false</em>.</span></li></ol><h4>Diagrams and Containment tree</h4><p>The following commands are available from the diagrams toolbar or from the shortcut menu of the supported elements in the Containment tree:</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 101.262%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 11.9235%;" /><col style="width: 88.0765%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p><strong>Button</strong></p></th><th><p><strong>Description</strong></p></th></tr><tr><th><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="22"><ri:attachment ri:filename="configure_target.png" /></ac:image> Configure Target</p></div></th><td><p>Configure the code generators according to the first operation you selected (this first operation will be your final target). You can manage the parameters of the generators here, as well as the download options.</p></td></tr><tr><th><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="23"><ri:attachment ri:filename="change_default_target.png" /></ac:image> Change Default Target</p></div></th><td><p>You can change the target selected for the current element.</p></td></tr><tr><th><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="code_generation.png" /></ac:image> Code Generation</p></div></th><td><p>Generates the code for the selected elements in, for example, the State Machine or Internal Block Definition diagrams. If the entire diagram is selected, the code is generated for the whole content.</p><p>The generation result is downloaded to your machine if you select the download option.</p><p>By default, the classic generation is triggered, but you can select the full generation to generate code in all circumstances.</p></td></tr><tr><th><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="23"><ri:attachment ri:filename="see_in_file.png" /></ac:image>Show in File Explorer</p></div></th><td><p>Open a location in your file system wherein the Software Production Operation results file is stored.</p></td></tr><tr><th><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="22"><ri:attachment ri:filename="roundtrip.png" /></ac:image>Roundtrip</p></div></th><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>The changes made in the generated code are applied in the State Machine diagram or Opaque Behavior.</p></div></td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Options</h3><p>You can <strong>deactivate the Software Production Operation</strong> you do not need.</p><p>To deactivate the Software Production Operation </p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu, click <strong>Options </strong>&gt; <strong>Environment</strong>. The <strong>Environment Options </strong>dialog opens.</li><li>In the dialog, go to <strong>CATIA Software Producer Client </strong>&gt; <strong>Operations</strong>.</li><li>Select the needed operation and click <strong>Change activation status.</strong></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>You can switch projects (or even shut down your computer, saving your work first) while waiting for the generation result as it is running on the cloud and as the widget keeps the IDs of the cloud assets in the record files located in &lt;local workspace path&gt;\etc\operations\spoRecordFile.json (you will have access to it if you need it, for example, for the debugging purpose). When you come back, another dialog will tell you that operations were running while you were away and will propose you inspect the result and download it.</p><h3>Additional Information</h3><p>A workspace is automatically created on our cloud servers for each of your projects using the widget. This workspace gathers the assets needed for the generation. A cloud workspace is automatically deleted after 30 days of inactivity. You can also delete it manually in the<strong> Environment Options </strong>dialog. If you delete the workspace, all the components will have to be regenerated on the cloud on your next session because you will not be able to push any files to a workspace through the plugin.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709707 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00172: CATIA Systems Synthesis

- page_id: `136709707`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709707/CATIA+Systems+Synthesis
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:04.805+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > CATIA applications
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1011081341

1011081353

1011081343

An integrated MDZipX plugin helps to improve the display of diagrams on the **3D**EXPERIENCE platform by converting them to the MDZipX file.

**Publish MDzipx file to Teamwork Cloud**command only works if you are logged in to Teamwork Cloud.If collaboration powered by the**3D**EXPERIENCE platform is enabled, you cannot work with Teamwork Cloud, so****the command is not available.

You can either generate the local .mdzipx file, or you can publish the .mdzipx file to **Teamwork Cloud**:

| Command | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Export to MDZipX File | The .mdzipx file contains:the .mdzip filethe .svg file for each diagram |
| Export to MDZipX File Including Data from Used Projects | The .mdzipx file contains:the .mdzip filethe .svg file for each diagramdata from used projects |
| Publish MDZipX File to Teamwork Cloud | For the published Teamwork Cloud project, the .svg file is saved for each diagram of each revision. |

To export to a local MDZipX

1. In the main menu, click 3DEXPERIENCE > CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis .
2. Select Export to MDZipX File . [ATTACHMENT filename='catia_systems_synthesis.png']
3. In the Select target directory dialog, specify the location for the MDZipX file on your file system and then click Save .

To export to a local MDZipX, including data from the used projects

1. In the main menu, click 3DEXPERIENCE > CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis .
2. Select Export to MDZipX File Including Data from Used Projects . [ATTACHMENT filename='export_to_mdzipx_with_used_projects.png']
3. In the Select target directory dialog, specify the location for the MDZipX file on your file system and then click Save .

To publish MDZipX to Teamwork Cloud

1. In the main menu, click 3DEXPERIENCE > CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis .
2. Select Publish MDZipX File to Teamwork Cloud . [ATTACHMENT filename='catia_systems synthesis_TWC.png']

For more information about the authentication with the platform, see the chapter [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Authentication with 3DEXPERIENCE platform' space='MD2024x'].

You can specify the diagram export options, such as dimensions and building time (the latter applies only to tables and matrices), in the **Project Options** dialog. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagram image export' space='MD2024x'] page to learn more.

##### Environment Options

You can enable the environment option to include diagrams from used projects when publishing MDZipX to Teamwork Cloud or exporting a local project into the MDZipX file. The MDZipX file is automatically published upon the project commit if the automatic MDZipX publishing is enabled.

To include diagrams from used projects when publishing MDZipX

1. Click Options > Environment .
2. In the panel on the left, select Collaboration .
3. Under CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis , select Publish Diagrams from Used Projects .
4. Click OK .

To enable the automatic MDZipX publishing

1. Click Options > Environment .
2. In the panel on the left, select Collaboration .
3. Under CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis , select Publish MDZipX Upon Commit .
4. Click OK .

For more information, please

MagicDraw and NoMagic Teamwork Cloud Connectors Configuration

1011081340

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Authentication with 3DEXPERIENCE platform' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="26ee2b7f-3702-4ad4-8ead-ea01bc2d8535"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1011081341</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="48bf6c12-9c03-41ea-a3ba-14455cdf7b3c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1011081353</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="832be424-0199-4463-8fe1-97f7b23bfbce"><ac:parameter available.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>You can either generate the local .mdzipx file, or you can publish the .mdzipx file to <strong>Teamwork Cloud</strong>:</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 34.0544%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 47.7977%;" /><col style="width: 52.2023%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Command</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td><strong>Export to MDZipX File</strong></td><td><p>The .mdzipx file contains:</p><ul><li>the .mdzip file</li><li>the .svg file for each diagram</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>Export to MDZipX File Including Data from Used Projects</strong></td><td colspan="1"><p>The .mdzipx file contains:</p><ul><li>the .mdzip file</li><li>the .svg file for each diagram</li><li>data from used projects</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td><strong>Publish MDZipX File to Teamwork Cloud</strong></td><td>For the published Teamwork Cloud project, the .svg file is saved for each diagram of each revision.</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p>To export to a local MDZipX</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu, click <strong>3DEXPERIENCE </strong>&gt; <strong>CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis</strong>.</li><li>Select <strong>Export to MDZipX File</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="catia_systems_synthesis.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="CATIA Systems Synthesis" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the<strong> Select target directory</strong> dialog, specify the location for the MDZipX file on your file system and then click <strong>Save</strong>.<br /><br /></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To export to a local MDZipX, including data from the used projects</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu, click <strong>3DEXPERIENCE </strong>&gt; <strong>CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis</strong>.</li><li>Select <strong>Export to MDZipX File Including Data from Used Projects</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="export_to_mdzipx_with_used_projects.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="CATIA Systems Synthesis" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the<strong> Select target directory</strong> dialog, specify the location for the MDZipX file on your file system and then click <strong>Save</strong>.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To publish MDZipX to Teamwork Cloud</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu, click <strong>3DEXPERIENCE </strong>&gt; <strong>CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis</strong>.</li><li>Select <strong>Publish MDZipX File to Teamwork Cloud</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="catia_systems synthesis_TWC.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="CATIA Systems Synthesis" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p>    </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0db3b9b4-68ed-4b35-923a-29a52d96bb8f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For more information about the authentication with the platform, see the chapter <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Authentication with 3DEXPERIENCE platform" /></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="519fb3a1-8e91-45c4-8872-407c6de756da"><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="17762c73-9d34-41b1-ae11-97e172ec99d7">You can specify the diagram export options, such as dimensions and building time (the latter applies only to tables and matrices), in the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog.</ac:inline-comment-marker> See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Diagram image export" /></ac:link> page to learn more.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Environment Options</h3><p>You can enable the environment option to include diagrams from used projects when publishing MDZipX to Teamwork Cloud or exporting a local project into the MDZipX file. The MDZipX file is automatically published upon the project commit if the automatic MDZipX publishing is enabled. </p><p><br /></p><p>To include diagrams from used projects when publishing MDZipX</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Environment</strong>. </li><li>In the panel on the left, select <strong>Collaboration</strong>. </li><li>Under<strong> CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis</strong>, select <strong>Publish Diagrams from Used Projects</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /></li></ol><p>To enable the automatic MDZipX publishing </p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Environment</strong>.</li><li>In the panel on the left, select <strong>Collaboration</strong>.</li><li>Under <strong>CATIA Systems Synthesis Analysis</strong>, select <strong>Publish MDZipX Upon Commit</strong>. </li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="671a27e2-5851-4cc8-899c-f76020a4f529"><ac:rich-text-body><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="efb3c36f-4ae1-4d74-ad08-c19a8e7c2527">For more information, please</ac:inline-comment-marker> refer to <a href="https://help.3ds.com/2023x/English/DSDoc/TraUserMap/smv-c-ad-NoMagicConfiguration.htm?contextscope=onpremise">MagicDraw and NoMagic Teamwork Cloud Connectors Configuration</a>.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="98343d2f-5c44-4976-8ec8-c5ac2d35b6ab"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1011081340</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Authentication with 3DEXPERIENCE platform" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00173: CG Properties Editor

- page_id: `136715646`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715646/CG+Properties+Editor
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:41.277+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > C++ Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### File Separator Format

This property is used to indicate the format of a file separator used in code generation.

##### Default Return Value

You can set the default return value to be used for generating function body for new function. For example, if the default return value of bool is set to false and generated, the function shown below will be created in a .cpp file.

```cpp

```

| Property Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Interactive mode | If enabled, you will be questioned during the reverse process when the parser cannot decide whether the symbol is a class or namespace. For example:If you reverse the following code class A { B::X i; }; The parser does not have enough information to decide whether B is a class or namespace. Figure 122 -- Question dialog for interactive mode Default ModeIf you are using the default mode, rather than the interactive mode, the reverse module will automatically give the best guess according to the information in the code. |
| Parse error before stop | Allows you to specify the number of errors to ignore before the reverse process will be terminated. You can set the property to zero to allow CE to reverse all code before stopping. |
| Parse Includes | If set to false, the reverse module will reverse only the selected files and ignore all #include statements. |
| Show message for not found includes | Used to display messages in the message window when the reverse module cannot find the files included in the #include statement. Parse IncludesYou must set the property Parse Includes to true in order to use the property Show message for not found includes. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
