<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715293 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00271: Create OCL2.0 validation rule

- page_id: `136715293`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715293/Create+OCL2.0+validation+rule
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:34:36.618+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Creating validation rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The OCL2.0 validation rule describes the validation logic using OCL2.0.

To create the OCL2.0 validation rule

1. Create a constraint.
2. Set the stereotype «UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile::validationRule» for the validation rule.
3. Set the severity level, error message, and abbreviation.
4. Specify constrained element(s).
5. Specify the specification language OCL2.0
6. Enter the OCL2.0 expression as a body of the specification
7. Add/import the created validation rule to a validation suite

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating validation rules' space='']

The OCL2.0 validation rule can be used in an active validation suite. In this case the validation rule will be executed on any change of constrained elements that are from the validation scope. Executing of the validation rule might be triggered even if no properties important to the validation rule actually changed and this can slow down the active validating speed. In order to avoid degradation of the performance you can create a binary validation rule that uses the OCL2.0 expression to validate model elements but also provides additional information for the modeling tool that allows to optimize triggering of executing after model elements change. For example, we want to check whether all classes have names. This can be done by creating the OCL2.0 validation rule and specifying the OLC2.0 expression:

```text
name <> ‘’
```

The problem is that such validation rule actually is interested in a class property name value change but it will be executed on every property value of a class change. How we can fix this problem and inform the modeling tool to execute the validation rule only on the class property name change:

1. Create a constraint.
2. Set the stereotype «UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile::validationRule» for the validation rule.
3. Set the severity level, error message, and abbreviation.
4. Specify constrained element(s).
5. Specify the specification language OCL2.0
6. Enter the OCL2.0 expression as a body of the specification
7. Create a Java class that extends the com.nomagic.magicdraw.validation.DefaultValidationRuleImpl class
8. Override the method DefaultValidationRuleImpl.getListenerConfigurations()

```java
, Collection> getListenerConfigurations()
        {
            Map, Collection> configs = 
						new HashMap, Collection>();
            Collection configsForClass = new ArrayList();
            configsForClass.add(SmartListenerConfig.NAME_CONFIG);
            configs.put(com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Class.class, configsForClass);
            return configs;
        }]]>
```

9. Specify the validation rule’s implementation property value - a qualified name of the class

10. Add/import the created validation rule to a validation suite

See the *MyOCLBasedValidationRuleImpl.java* example in the *<program installation directory>\openapi\examples\validation* directory.

The implementation class must be in the classpath of the modeling tool or if the implementation class is in a plugin then the plugin’s classloader must be registered to the validation system (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Binary validation rule in the plugin' space='']).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>The OCL2.0 validation rule describes the validation logic using OCL2.0.</p><p>To create the OCL2.0 validation rule</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a constraint.</li><li>Set the stereotype «UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile::validationRule» for the validation rule.</li><li>Set the severity level, error message, and abbreviation.</li><li>Specify constrained element(s).</li><li>Specify the specification language OCL2.0</li><li>Enter the OCL2.0 expression as a body of the specification</li><li>Add/import the created validation rule to a validation suite</li></ol></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f368d5fe-805a-447b-a01b-fca122ddd589"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating validation rules" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><div class="WordSection1"><p>The OCL2.0 validation rule can be used in an active validation suite. In this case the validation rule will be executed on any change of constrained elements that are from the validation scope. Executing of the validation rule might be triggered even if no properties important to the validation rule actually changed and this can slow down the active validating speed. In order to avoid degradation of the performance you can create a binary validation rule that uses the OCL2.0 expression to validate model elements but also provides additional information for the modeling tool that allows to optimize triggering of executing after model elements change. For example,  we want to check whether all classes have names. This can be done by creating the OCL2.0 validation rule and specifying the OLC2.0 expression:</p></div><pre>    name &lt;&gt; ‘’</pre><p>The problem is that such validation rule actually is interested in a class property name value change but it will be executed on every property value of a class change. How we can fix this problem and inform the modeling tool to execute the validation rule only on the class property name change:</p><ol><li>Create a constraint.</li><li>Set the stereotype «UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile::validationRule» for the validation rule.</li><li>Set the severity level, error message, and abbreviation.</li><li>Specify constrained element(s).</li><li>Specify the specification language OCL2.0</li><li>Enter the OCL2.0 expression as a body of the specification</li><li>Create a Java class that extends the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.validation.DefaultValidationRuleImpl</em> class</li><li>Override the method<em> DefaultValidationRuleImpl.getListenerConfigurations()</em></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="973fd9f7-00bc-4430-9d30-eb80eb0615b2"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[        public Map<Class<? extends Element>, Collection<SmartListenerConfig>> getListenerConfigurations()
        {
            Map<Class<? extends Element>, Collection<SmartListenerConfig>> configs = 
						new HashMap<Class<? extends Element>, Collection<SmartListenerConfig>>();
            Collection<SmartListenerConfig> configsForClass = new ArrayList<SmartListenerConfig>();
            configsForClass.add(SmartListenerConfig.NAME_CONFIG);
            configs.put(com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Class.class, configsForClass);
            return configs;
        }]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>    9. Specify the validation rule’s implementation property value - a qualified name of the class</p><p>    10. Add/import the created validation rule to a validation suite</p><p><br /></p><p>See the <em>MyOCLBasedValidationRuleImpl.java</em> example in the <em>&lt;program installation directory&gt;\openapi\examples\validation</em> directory.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="32f9080c-2334-45c1-b680-ee01eae87bd7"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The implementation class must be in the classpath of the modeling tool or if the implementation class is in a plugin then the plugin’s classloader must be registered to the validation system (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Binary validation rule in the plugin" /></ac:link>).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711417 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00272: Creating a basic flow

- page_id: `136711417`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711417/Creating+a+basic+flow
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T10:14:04.981+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Use Case > Use Case Scenario sketch
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

795872164

795872166

795872165

INLINE

To create a basic flow

1. Open the Use Case Specification window and click the Use Case Scenario Sketch property group.
2. In the Basic Flow area, click the Add new step button [ATTACHMENT filename='add_new_value_button.png'] or press Alt+ENTER and type a name of the basic flow step. Repeat this action for each basic flow step you need to create.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Steps of the basic flow are numbered automatically. Use the Up [IMAGE alt='' src='']and Down [IMAGE alt='' src=''] buttons to change the order of steps. In the activity diagram, steps are represented according to their order in the Use Case scenario.

795872163

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='MD2024x']

<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95943085-9713-4722-b81f-cabff034c66b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">795872164</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b2d0119-796a-44ac-9226-0cc70f7bd30b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">795872166</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d07a9954-bb89-4537-a41c-830112635c85"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">795872165</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="025add5e-4dc0-475f-b48f-2511d727e973"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To create a basic flow</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Use Case Specification window and click the <strong>Use Case Scenario Sketch </strong>property group.</li><li>In the <strong>Basic Flow </strong>area, click the Add new step button <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_new_value_button.png" /></ac:image> or press Alt+ENTER and type a name of the basic flow step. In the activity diagram, steps are represented according to their order in the Use Case scenario.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="093459a2-0bf2-4020-9a33-7f585c68705f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">795872163</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Transition" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715247 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00273: Creating a custom dependency matrix type

- page_id: `136715247`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715247/Creating+a+custom+dependency+matrix+type
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:15:47.336+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Working with diagrams > Working with Dependency Matrix
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a custom dependency matrix type:

1. Create a new plugin to register components of the new dependency matrix type.
2. Register the new dependency matrix type that it was added to modeling tool toolbars and menus.

###### **creatingCreatin****g a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Plugins' space=''] for custom dependency matrix type**

Use the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.configuration.DependencyMatrixConfigurator* class to register components of the new dependency matrix type as shown in the following example:

```java

```

Remember the name of the new dependency matrix type that you pass as an argument while creating a new instance of the *DependencyMatrixConfigurator* class (in the preceding example, it is “Sample Extended Matrix”). You will have to specify the exact name when registering a new diagram type.

The sample of more detailed configuration code is provided in *<program installation folder>\openapi\examples\dependencymatrix*.

###### **registeringRegisterin****g a new dependency matrix type in UI**

Once the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Plugins' space='']is created, you have to register the new dependency matrix type by using the **Customize****Dependency Matrix Wizard**dialog. Only then it will be added to program toolbars and menus.

To register a new dependency matrix type

1. Start a modeling tool, for example, MagicDraw.
2. On the Diagrams menu, click Customize . The Customize Diagrams dialog opens.
3. Click Create and then select Dependency Matrix Type . The Customize Dependency Matrix W izard dialog opens.
4. In the Type box, specify the name of the new dependency matrix type. The value must be the same as the one passed to the DependencyMatrixConfigurator class as the argument. [ATTACHMENT filename='CustomizeMatrixWizard.png']

*Specifying name of new Dependency Matrix type*

5. In the **Abbreviation**box, specify the abbreviation of the new dependency matrix type.

6. Click **Finish**.

7. Restart your modeling tool.

You can now create a custom dependency matrix.

For more information on how to customize a new dependency matrix type by using the **Customize Dependency Matrix Wizard**dialog, **see “Creating New Dependency Matrix Type” in theMagicDraw UMLProfiling&DSL UserGuide.pdf.**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><div class="WordSection2"><p>To create a custom dependency matrix type:</p><ol><li>Create a new plugin to register components of the new dependency matrix type.</li><li>Register the new dependency matrix type that it was added to modeling tool toolbars and menus.</li></ol><h4><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4acdf11c-df7d-48f8-ba8f-7dd4872ba3d1"><ac:parameter ac:name="">creating</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Creatin</strong><strong>g a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Plugins" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[plugin]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for custom dependency matrix type</strong></h4><p>Use the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.dependencymatrix.configuration.DependencyMatrixConfigurator</em> class to register components of the new dependency matrix type as shown in the following example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a5fabd52-a09e-41d8-81e2-5269fa22d466"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    public class DependencyMatrixExample extends Plugin
    {
        @Override
        public void init()
        {
            DependencyMatrixConfigurator.registerConfiguration(new DependencyMatrixSampleConfigurator("Sample Extended Matrix"));
        }
 
        @Override
        public boolean close()
        {
            return true;
        }
 
        @Override
        public boolean isSupported()
        {
            return true;
        }
    }]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c1f58ccb-07d4-45bb-846c-efc41fe67055"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Remember the name of the new dependency matrix type that you pass as an argument while creating a new instance of the <em>DependencyMatrixConfigurator</em> class (in the preceding example, it is “Sample Extended Matrix”). You will have to specify the exact name when registering a new diagram type.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The sample of more detailed configuration code is provided in <em>&lt;program installation folder&gt;\openapi\examples\dependencymatrix</em>.</p><p><br /></p><h4><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a7c489e5-8d62-4b31-a038-712ade005703"><ac:parameter ac:name="">registering</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Registerin</strong><strong>g a new dependency matrix type in UI</strong></h4><p>Once the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Plugins" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[plugin ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>is created, you have to register the new dependency matrix type by using the <strong>Customize </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Dependency Matrix Wizard </strong>dialog. Only then it will be added to program toolbars and menus.</p><p>To register a new dependency matrix type</p><hr /><ol><li>Start a modeling tool, for example, MagicDraw.</li><li>On the <strong>Diagrams </strong>menu, click <strong>Customize</strong>. The <strong>Customize Diagrams </strong>dialog opens.</li><li>Click <strong>Create </strong>and then select <strong>Dependency Matrix Type</strong>. The <strong>Customize Dependency Matrix </strong><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">W</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">izard </strong>dialog opens.</li><li>In the <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Type </strong>box, specify the name of the new dependency matrix type. The value must be the same as the one passed to the <em>DependencyMatrixConfigurator</em> class as the argument.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="CustomizeMatrixWizard.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><em>Specifying name of new Dependency Matrix type</em></p><p>    5. In the <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Abbreviation </strong>box, specify the abbreviation of the new dependency matrix type.</p><p>    6. Click <strong>Finish</strong>.</p><p>    7. Restart your modeling tool.</p><p>You can now create a custom dependency matrix.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="384e72cb-62bb-4d09-a413-c5bc01cfaf26"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For more information on how to customize a new dependency matrix type by using the <strong>Customize Dependency Matrix Wizard </strong>dialog, <strong><span style="color: rgb(255,0,0);">see “Creating New Dependency Matrix Type” in the<u> MagicDraw </u> <u>UMLProfiling&amp;DSL UserGuide.pdf</u>.</span></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715194 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00274: Creating a diagram

- page_id: `136715194`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715194/Creating+a+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:11.774+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Working with diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Every diagram has a diagram type and this type should be provided during diagram creation.

An example shows how to create and add a diagram to a parent element:

```java

```

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Session management' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><div class="WordSection1"><div>Every diagram has a diagram type and this type should be provided during diagram creation.</div><div>An example shows how to create and add a diagram to a parent element:</div><div><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7545b1a6-1ac5-499e-9b55-35faef700a9b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    Project project = ...;
    Package parent = project.getPrimaryModel();
    // create a new session
    SessionManager.getInstance().createSession(project, "Create a diagram");
    try
    {
        //a class diagram is created and added to a parent model element
        Diagram diagram = ModelElementsManager.getInstance().createDiagram(DiagramTypeConstants.UML_CLASS_DIAGRAM, parent);
        //open a diagram 
        project.getDiagram(diagram).open();
    }
    catch (ReadOnlyElementException e)
    {
    }
    SessionManager.getInstance().closeSession(project);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></div></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f7849a87-66ab-4f1a-96c8-df51caad3ac7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Session management" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710504 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00275: Creating a diagram overview shape

- page_id: `136710504`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710504/Creating+a+diagram+overview+shape
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:42.727+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Overviewing other diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

569216953

569216957

569216955

You can use one of the following ways to create a diagram overview shape:

- Drag the diagram shape from the Model Browser and change it into the diagram overview shape afterward . This way created diagram overview shape shows the full content of the corresponding diagram and is updated automatically according to all changes made in this diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='dgr_and_dgr_overview_shape_full.png']
- **Use the [IMAGE alt='' src='']Diagram Overview button that is located in the diagram palette**. This way created diagram overview shape shows the full content of the corresponding diagram and is updated automatically according to all changes made in this diagram.
- **Paste a copied part of the diagram’s content using a special command from the main menu**. This way created diagram overview shape shows any copied part of the corresponding diagram’s content and is updated automatically according only to *the changes made in this copied part*. Note that this way cannot be used for dependency matrices, tables, and relation maps, as it is not allowed to copy and paste different parts of them. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create a diagram overview shape by dragging it to the diagram pane

1. Open a diagram wherein you want to create the diagram overview shape.
2. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024x'] select a diagram you want to overview.
3. Drag the diagram shape to the open diagram pane. If you cannot drag the diagram shape to the open diagram pane when working with a Teamwork Cloud project, make sure you have the right to edit the model of this project and then try to lock for edit this diagram.
4. Make the diagram shape show the full content of the corresponding diagram by doing one of the following:
  - Right-click the diagram shape, and from the shortcut menu, select [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024x'] . In the open dialog, set the Show Diagram Overview Content property value to true . [ATTACHMENT filename='show_dgr_overview_content_symbol_properties.png']
  - Click the diagram shape, and on the smart manipulator toolbar, select the [IMAGE alt='' src='']button. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The diagram shape will be changed to the diagram overview shape that shows the full content of the corresponding diagram. The content of the diagram overview shape will be updated automatically according to*all changes* made in the corresponding diagram. Double-click the diagram overview shape to open the corresponding diagram.

To create a diagram overview shape by using the diagram palette

1. Open a diagram wherein you want to create the diagram overview shape.
2. On the diagram palette, click the **Diagram Overview** button that is located within the **Common** buttons group. The **Select Diagram** dialog is open. If the diagram palette is inactive within a Teamwork Cloud project, make sure you have the right to edit the model of this project and then try to lock for edit this diagram.
3. Select a diagram for which you want to create a diagram overview shape and click OK .
4. Click a free space of the diagram pane.

To create a diagram overview shape by pasting any part of the diagram’s content

1. Select the element shapes that you want to appear in the diagram overview shape and then copy them.
2. Open the diagram wherein you want to create the diagram overview shape.
3. From the **Edit** menu, select **Paste as Diagram Overview**. The content will be updated automatically according to the changes made in the copied part of the corresponding diagram only.

569216952

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying the content of a diagram overview shape' space='MD185']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a8c95829-f72d-4341-a70e-b9c92708f88e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">569216953</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9a18e002-1a9a-4b23-9eb8-cb4eb2ed8677"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">569216957</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="12a9ec13-7e63-409f-8107-ddb1bd4a3ae5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">569216955</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can use one of the following ways to create a diagram overview shape:</p><ul><li><strong>Drag the diagram shape from the Model Browser and change it into the diagram overview shape afterward</strong>. This way created diagram overview shape shows the full content of the corresponding diagram and is updated automatically according to all changes made in this diagram.<br /><ac:image ac:title="An example of diagram and its diagram overview shape showing the full content of the diagram" ac:alt="An example of diagram and its diagram overview shape showing the full content of the diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dgr_and_dgr_overview_shape_full.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a diagram overview shape" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </li><li><p><strong>Use the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="diagram_overview.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a diagram overview shape" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Diagram Overview button that is located in the diagram palette</strong>. This way created diagram overview shape shows the full content of the corresponding diagram and is updated automatically according to all changes made in this diagram. </p></li><li><p><strong>Paste a copied part of the diagram’s content using a special command from the main menu</strong>. This way created diagram overview shape shows any copied part of the corresponding diagram’s content and is updated automatically according only to <em>the changes made in this copied part</em>. Note that this way cannot be used for dependency matrices, tables, and relation maps, as it is not allowed to copy and paste different parts of them.<br /><ac:image ac:title="An example of diagram and its diagram overview shape showing only the part of the diagram content" ac:alt="An example of diagram and its diagram overview shape showing only the part of the diagram content"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dgr_and_dgr_overview_shape_part.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a diagram overview shape" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </p></li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To create a diagram overview shape by dragging it to the diagram pane</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a diagram wherein you want to create the diagram overview shape.</li><li>In the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Model Browser" /></ac:link></strong> select a diagram you want to overview.</li><li><p>Drag the diagram shape to the open diagram pane.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07f8d55a-b7b3-45bb-bd6e-70cfde831e0c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you cannot drag the diagram shape to the open diagram pane when working with a Teamwork Cloud project, make sure you have the right to edit the model of this project and then try to lock for edit this diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Make the diagram shape show the full content of the corresponding diagram by doing one of the following:<ul><li>Right-click the diagram shape, and from the shortcut menu, select <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Symbol Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong>. In the open dialog, set the <strong>Show Diagram Overview Content</strong> property value to <em>true</em>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="show_dgr_overview_content_symbol_properties.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a diagram overview shape" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><p>Click the diagram shape, and on the smart manipulator toolbar, select the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="show_dgr_overview_content_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a diagram overview shape" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>button.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="show_dgr_overview_content.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a diagram overview shape" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The diagram shape will be changed to the diagram overview shape that shows the full content of the corresponding diagram.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a268ba55-34d5-483c-aefe-67bb081ec3ff"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The content of the diagram overview shape will be updated automatically according to<em> all changes</em> made in the corresponding diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="61cce830-fc71-418e-8c98-b09ad3fd38ee"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Double-click the diagram overview shape to open the corresponding diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create a diagram overview shape by using the diagram palette</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a diagram wherein you want to create the diagram overview shape.</li><li><p>On the diagram palette, click the <strong>Diagram Overview</strong> button that is located within the <strong>Common</strong> buttons group. The <strong>Select Diagram</strong> dialog is open.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4403312c-45d3-4539-b476-bfe94f4e2d99"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the diagram palette is inactive within a Teamwork Cloud project, make sure you have the right to edit the model of this project and then try to lock for edit this diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Select a diagram for which you want to create a diagram overview shape and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Click a free space of the diagram pane.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create a diagram overview shape by pasting any part of the diagram’s content</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the element shapes that you want to appear in the diagram overview shape and then copy them.</li><li>Open the diagram wherein you want to create the diagram overview shape.</li><li><p>From the <strong>Edit</strong> menu, select <strong>Paste as Diagram Overview</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a8b3cfe0-8867-4dc9-a342-097ce8c7f82c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The content will be updated automatically according to the changes made in the copied part of the corresponding diagram only.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8f6794db-50f7-447c-ad58-a997e8aa7a3b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">569216952</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD185" ri:content-title="Modifying the content of a diagram overview shape" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714979 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00276: Creating a DSL Element from the Customized Category in the Shortcut Menu

- page_id: `136714979`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714979/Creating+a+DSL+Element+from+the+Customized+Category+in+the+Shortcut+Menu
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:59.306+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **Create Element** menu in the Model Browser allows you to group elements into categories. You can also use various menus in dialogs and specifications to group elements. Use the **Category** property of a customization element to create categories.

**Case Study**

The following example demonstrates how to create a category for a new requirement element within a Package element. This example refers to the project described in [Case Study: Creating Your First Customization](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+your+First+Customization).

1. Assign the custom stereotype (in this case the *«Company»* stereotype) as a **Customization Target** property in the customization element Specification window. Specify the Package metaclass as a **Possible Owners** property value. Please see the [Case Study: Creating Your First Customization](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+your+First+Customization) for more details.
2. In the customization element Specification window: [IMAGE alt='' src=''] In this example, we used the stereotype created in the example described in [Case Study: Creating Your First Customization](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+your+First+Customization).
  - specify the name of a new category as the **Category** property value (for example, *Requirements)*.
  - specify the name of an element to create as the **Representation text**property value (for example, *Business Requirements)*.
3. Save and reopen your project.
4. The *Business Requirement* element is added to the package shortcut menu, as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The custom Requirements category in the **Create Element**menu.

To create several items under the same category, you must create a customization element for each item. Specify the same name of the **Category** property.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714986 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00277: Creating a DSL Element from the Shortcut Menu and Other Locations

- page_id: `136714986`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714986/Creating+a+DSL+Element+from+the+Shortcut+Menu+and+Other+Locations
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:59.577+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

220200288

220200291

220200290

The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating your First Customization' space='MD2024x'] section introduced the step-by-step process of creating a custom element. Following are tips to create custom elements.

Use the **Possible Owners** property to define the possible owner for a customized element. You can also quickly create a customized element from the possible owner shortcut menu in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024x'] and in other locations. For example, you can add a package as a possible owner for a customized use case element.

Use the **Possible Owners** property to quickly create a customized element from the following locations:

- The possible owner’s shortcut menu, under the Create Element command, in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024x']
- The Inner Elements property group under the possible owner’s Specification window.
- The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting elements' space='MD2024x']**Select Element** dialog , when the element creation mode is turned on.

Examples of creating the *Business Requirement*element in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a DSL Element from the Customized Category in the Shortcut Menu' space='MD2024x'] are shown below:

- In the package shortcut menu (in the Model Browser) under the Create Element command. [ATTACHMENT filename='package_shortcut_menu.png']
- In the possible owner’s Specification window, the Inner Elements property group. [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_in_specification_window.png']
- In the element selection dialog, when creating a new elem ent. [ATTACHMENT filename='select_owner_dialog_creation_mode.png']

220200287

**Related Pages**

- Creating Customization Data
- Using Customization Data
- Customization Element Properties Description

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715675 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00278: Creating a Macro

- page_id: `136715675`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715675/Creating+a+Macro
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:41.900+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you have selected a default macro language (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting a default macro language' space='MD2024x']), you can create a new macro by using the **Create Macro** dialog, see figure below. The dialog allows you to specify a macro language, enter source code, and save it.

To create a new macro

1. Click Tools > Macros > Create Macro on the main menu. The Create Macro dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='create-macro.png']
2. Select a macro language in the **Macro Language** box. To ensure the best performance experience, it is strongly suggested that you use the recommended language (Groovy). The icon color next to the language selection reflects performance, (i.e., green for fast, yellow for medium, and red for slow). If the performance is unknown, an info icon appears.
3. Enter the source code in the text box.
4. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to test and make sure that the source code works properly.
5. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] . The Macro Information dialog opens. Follow the steps described on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding a Macro and editing Macro information' space='MD2024x'] page.
6. After clicking OK in the Macro Information dialog, the new macro will be saved in the location you have specified in the File text box.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Once you have selected a default macro language (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Selecting a default macro language" /></ac:link>), you can create a new macro by using the <strong>Create Macro</strong> dialog, see figure below. The dialog allows you to specify a macro language, enter source code, and save it.</p><p>To create a new macro</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Macros</strong> &gt; <strong>Create Macro</strong> on the main menu. The<strong> Create Macro</strong> dialog opens.<br /><ac:image ac:title="The Create Macro Dialog" ac:alt="The Create Macro Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="create-macro.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a Macro" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"> Select a macro language in the <strong>Macro Language</strong> box.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="08c8f1bf-cf76-4a9e-a576-3bba18f3c988"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To ensure the best performance experience, it is strongly suggested that you use the recommended language (Groovy). The icon color next to the language selection reflects performance, (i.e., green for fast, yellow for medium, and red for slow). If the performance is unknown, an info icon appears.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li> Enter the source code in the text box.</li><li> Click <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="run-btn.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a Macro" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong> to test and make sure that the source code works properly.</li><li> Click <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="save-btn.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a Macro" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong>. The <strong>Macro Information</strong> dialog opens. Follow the steps described on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Adding a Macro and Its Information]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li><li> After clicking <strong>OK</strong> in the <strong>Macro Information</strong> dialog, the new macro will be saved in the location you have specified in the <strong>File</strong> text box.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710396 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00279: Creating a Metric Table

- page_id: `136710396`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710396/Creating+a+Metric+Table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:37.436+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables > Metric table
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Although you can create other types of tables in various ways, there is only one correct way to create a Metric Table. This method of creating Metric Tables saves much time compared to the more common ways of table (and even diagram) creation, as these methods for a Metric Table creation start with an empty table. This requires further effort on your part to fill it (specify the metric suite, create an empty row, define parameters, and then calculate the metrics).

To create a Metric Table

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space='MD2024x'], right-click thePackage which contains e.g. requirements, system structure, system behavior, which will be used as a scope for the metrics calculation.
2. From the shortcut menu of the element, select **Tools** > **Metrics** > **New Metric Table**.
3. In the **Create Metric Table** dialog, specify the following: 
 - Type the Metric Table name; 
 - Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to specify the Metric Table owner.By default, the selected Package in the Containment tree is chosen. 
 - From the list of available metric suites, select the check boxes next to the Metric Suites you want to use.There are three groups of predefined Metric Suites: If predefined Metric Suites do not allow to evaluate the current state of your model, you can create a custom Metric Suite according to your needs.[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Metric Suites' space='MD2024x']>]]> [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Metric Suite descriptionsSelect a Metric Suite and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to read its description in the description area of the **Create Metric Table** dialog. Carefully read descriptions of each metric suite. This helps you decide which one best suits your needs.
  - Requirement Metric Suites (Obsolete)
  - Requirement Coverage (Treat Owner As Grouping Element)
  - Requirement Coverage (Treat Owner As Requirement)
4. Click **OK**.

The table of a particular Metric Suite is created for the selected Package. It is already filled with data because metrics calculated at the time you create the table. These metrics are actually stored in the Package owned by the element for which they have been calculated. As example, see the figure below. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

If one or more metrics are not calculated, make sure the Metric Suite is specified correctly:

- Check the metric definition – the formula could be specified incorrectly.
- Check the parameter definition – the metric definition probably cannot access parameter values.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Although you can create other types of tables in various ways, there is only one correct way to create a Metric Table. This method of creating Metric Tables saves much time compared to the more common ways of table (and even diagram) creation, as these methods for a Metric Table creation start with an empty table. This requires further effort on your part to fill it (specify the metric suite, create an empty row, define parameters, and then calculate the metrics).</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a Metric requirements, system structure, system behavior, which will be used as a scope for the metrics calculation.</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p></li><li><p>From the shortcut menu of the element, ac:ref="af912331-d680-4976-a2f3-3633cfa34063"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="af912331-d680-4976-a2f3-3633cfa34063"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="00838499-6238-41e5-9daf-c08c88bd0011">Requirement Metric Suites (Obsolete)</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="af912331-d680-4976-a2f3-3633cfa34063"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="af912331-d680-4976-a2f3-3633cfa34063"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="00838499-6238-41e5-9daf-c08c88bd0011">Requirement Coverage (Treat Owner As Grouping Element)</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="af912331-d680-4976-a2f3-3633cfa34063"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="af912331-d680-4976-a2f3-3633cfa34063"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="00838499-6238-41e5-9daf-c08c88bd0011">Requirement Coverage (Treat Owner As Requirement)</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></p></li></ul><div><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ff35542d-a390-4ac3-b6aa-762fc025b273"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If predefined Metric Suites do not allow <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">to evaluate the current state of your model</span>, you can create a custom Metric Suite according to your needs.<ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Metric Suites" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ Learn how to create a custom Metric Suite >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></div><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_metric_table_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a Metric Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33d924d6-176e-429b-81a8-10ca59948e57"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Metric Suite descriptions</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Select a Metric Suite and click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="17"><ri:attachment ri:filename="show_description.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a Metric Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to read its description in the description area of the <strong>Create Metric Table</strong> dialog. Carefully read descriptions of each metric suite. This helps you decide which one best suits your needs.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"> Click <strong>OK</strong>.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p class="auto-cursor-target">The table of a particular Metric Suite is created for the selected Package. It is already filled with data because metrics calculated at the time you create the table. These metrics are actually stored in the Package owned by the element for which they have been calculated. As example, see the figure below.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="requirements_satisfaction_metric_table.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a Metric Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bb30ad46-db45-49f9-9a27-dc57483b81ff"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If one or more metrics are not calculated, make sure the Metric Suite is specified correctly:</p><ul><li>Check the metric definition – the formula could be specified incorrectly.</li><li>Check the parameter definition – the metric definition probably cannot access parameter values.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709346 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00280: Creating a new project

- page_id: `136709346`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709346/Creating+a+new+project
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:11:44.427+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Creating projects
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1428335651

1428335654

1428335653

INLINE

All project information is stored in a single file that has the same name as the project itself.

A newly created project consists of the following packages:

- The empty root package Model, storing all model elements.
- File View package, containing components that are created during code engineering and representing source files. Adding a resident element to a particular component causes that element to be generated within the source file.
- UML Standard Profile, containing stereotypes that are necessary in order to work with MagicDraw, primitive data types and constraints (which are UML standard), and UML 2 metamodel elements. The following data types are specified in MagicDraw: boolean, byte, char, date, double, float, int, Integer, real long, short, void, and string.

You can create your own packages for keeping model elements as well. By default, packages cannot be deleted or renamed in a project (except for the *File View* package).

To start a new project, first, you must create a new workspace for it. You can select a project type from the following domains:

- General-Purpose Modeling (UML, Use Case, Guide to UML Diagrams projects)
- System Engineering (SysML project)
- Enterprise Modeling (DoDAF, DoDAF 2.0, MODAF projects)
- Software Engineering (Scrum project, Project from Existing Source Code)
- Business Process Modeling (BPMN 2.0 project)
- Service-Oriented Modeling (Cameo SOA+ project)
- Other (Project from Template, Process Guide project)

Upon creation of a new project, the **General-Purpose Modeling** domain opens by default.

To create a new workspace for a blank project

1. Do one of the following:

- 
  - From the File menu, select New Project .
  - On the main toolbar, click the New Project button.
  - Press Ctrl + Shift + N.

Any of the above actions opens the **New Project** dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

2. Select the **UML Project** in the **General-Purpose Modeling** domain.

3. Specify the file name in the **Name** box.

4. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button to select the location to store the created project.

5. Click **OK**.

1428335648

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating projects' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project Properties dialog' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with multiple projects' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0a6e6cce-a570-4e91-a4ad-f2902e860a19"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1428335651</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="04ef58d3-bd3a-4bee-94c4-b65f1a51fac7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1428335654</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="de1cbaad-8860-40df-b87c-320dc0b7c382"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1428335653</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7c6f51e9-ae1e-45cf-8e55-ca23f49421e9"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="atl-forced-newline">All project information is stored in a single file that has the same name as the project itself.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline">A newly created project consists of the following packages:</p><ul><li class="atl-forced-newline">The empty root package <em>Model,</em> storing all model elements.</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><em>File View</em> package, containing components that are created during code engineering and representing source files. Adding a resident element to a particular component causes that element to be generated within the source file.</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><em>UML Standard Profile,</em> containing stereotypes that are necessary in order to work with MagicDraw, primitive data types and constraints (which are UML standard), and UML 2 metamodel elements. The following data types are specified in MagicDraw: boolean, byte, char, date, double, float, int, Integer, real long, short, void, and string.</li></ul><p class="atl-forced-newline">You can create your own packages for keeping model elements as well. By default, packages cannot be deleted or renamed in a project (except for the <em>File View</em> package).</p><p class="atl-forced-newline">To start a new project, first, you must create a new workspace for it. You can select a project type from the following domains:</p><ul><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>General-Purpose Modeling</strong> (UML, Use Case, Guide to UML Diagrams projects)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>System Engineering</strong> (SysML project)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Enterprise Modeling</strong> (DoDAF, DoDAF 2.0, MODAF projects)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Software Engineering</strong> (Scrum project, Project from Existing Source Code)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Business Process Modeling</strong> (BPMN 2.0 project)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Service-Oriented Modeling</strong> (Cameo SOA+ project)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Other</strong> (Project from Template, Process Guide project)</li></ul><p class="atl-forced-newline">Upon creation of a new project, the <strong>General-Purpose Modeling</strong> domain opens by default.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline"><br /></p><p class="atl-forced-newline">To create a new workspace for a blank project</p><hr /><ol><li class="atl-forced-newline">Do one of the following:</li></ol><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li class="atl-forced-newline">From the <strong>File</strong> menu, select <strong>New Project</strong>.</li><li class="atl-forced-newline">On the main toolbar, click the <strong>New Project</strong> button.</li><li class="atl-forced-newline">Press Ctrl + Shift + N.</li></ul></li></ul><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Any of the above actions opens the <strong>New Project</strong> dialog.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline"><ac:image ac:title="New Project dialog" ac:alt="New Project dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="new_project_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a new project" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </p><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 30.0px;">2. Select the <strong>UML Project</strong> in the <strong>General-Purpose Modeling</strong> domain.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 30.0px;">3. Specify the file name in the <strong>Name</strong> box.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 30.0px;">4. Click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3dot_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button to select the location to store the created project.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 30.0px;">5. Click <strong>OK</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2476cc15-2c7a-46a2-938a-95f5957aa355"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1428335648</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating projects" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Project Properties dialog" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Working with multiple projects" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714550 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00281: Creating a new slide for a presentation

- page_id: `136714550`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714550/Creating+a+new+slide+for+a+presentation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:40.833+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Report Wizard Directives > #forpage directive
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a new slide for a presentation

1. Open OpenOffice.org Impress or Microsoft PowerPoint.
2. Create two text boxes.
3. Type in: 
(i) one of the text boxes: text (ii) and type in the other text box: text The result will appear as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The result of the generated report will appear shown in the figure below. 
 
 [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a new slide for a presentation</p><hr /><ol><li>Open <a href="http://OpenOffice.org">OpenOffice.org</a> Impress or Microsoft PowerPoint.</li><li>Create two text boxes.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Type in:<br />(i) one of the text boxes:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="94c10f12-41cb-4d4d-a942-9a270ff64ad8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#forpage($dia in $Diagram) 
$dia.name]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">(ii) and type in the other text box:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a112c55c-f1ea-4b2b-9b37-d8c22d0489ae"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$dia.image 
#endpage]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The result will appear as shown in the figure below.</p></li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavbf2e5609b8cf026cfb71c63ca946bfca.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a new slide for a presentation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The result of the generated report will appear shown in the figure below.<br /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <ac:image ac:title="A Generated Report" ac:alt="A Generated Report"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav8dfe469fffe291fde883d6b38b0573a7.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a new slide for a presentation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710643 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00282: Creating a relationship

- page_id: `136710643`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710643/Creating+a+relationship
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:49.388+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Working with paths and relationships
- labels: ['create-relationship', 'create-path', 'relationship-direction', 'relate-source-and-target', 'path-direction', 'path-target']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

You can create relationships between elements in one of the following ways:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a relationship' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a relationship' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a relationship' space='MD2024x']

diagram_paneTo draw a path on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space='MD2024x']

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space='MD2024x'] , click the needed relationship.
2. Select the source shape of the path in the diagram pane.
3. Drop the path to the target shape. When drawing a new path, the optimal route is chosen automatically (the path avoids other shapes).When drawing a path between two elements, those shapes are bordered with a red or blue rectangle. The red highlight indicates that the path can not be drawn between these shapes. The blue highlight allows a path to be drawn. You can disable this highlighting in the **Environment Options** dialog under the Diagram node by setting the **Show Manipulation Highlighting** property value to *false*. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing environment options' space='MD2024x'].To draw a path faster, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart manipulator toolbar' space='MD2024x'].

model_browserTo create relationships between selected elements

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024x'] , diagram pane, or table, right-click an element.
2. Choose Create Relation > Outgoing / Incoming and select the desired relationship type. The Create New <relation name> To / From dialog opens.
3. Do one or more of the following:
  - In the Model Browser, click the element to / from which you want to create a relationship.
  - On a diagram, click an element to / from which you want to create a relationship.
  - In the **Create New <relation name> To** /**From** dialog, click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. In the opened **Create New <relation name> To**/**From**dialog, choose the elementto / from which you want to create a relationship. If you do not know the exact location of the needed element, you can search for it in this step.
4. Click OK .

The **Active Selection** area shows the elements currently selected in the active diagram, table, matrix, map, or Model Browser. It changes with each click of the different elements when you switch to another diagram.

Tochoose one or several elements at a time,do one of the following

- Press Shift and click as many elements as you need.
- Click and drag the mouse pointer to select as many elements as you need.

The multiple selection works in one area at a time. For example, diagram pane, table, matrix, relation map, or Model Browser. If you need to select elements from several locations, you need to pin your current selection.

The **Pinned Selection** area shows the elements you choose to pin from the **Active Selection**. This is needed when you want to select elements from different locations (for example, you need to select elements from different diagrams, or from the diagram and Model Browser). In this case, you should pin each selection.

specificationTo create a relationship in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']

1. Open the element's Specification window and select the Relations property group.
2. Click the Create Outgoing button or Create Incoming button and then select the desired relation type from the list.
3. In the Create New <relation name> To/ From dialog select an element to/ from which you want to create a relation and click OK . The relation appears in the Relations property group.

When the relationship is created, you can visually identify its direction (source and target elements) under the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space='MD2024x'] >**Relations**.

The arrow between source and target elements appears if the path is created using only directed relationships.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can create relationships between elements in one of the following ways: </p><ul><li><span class="confluence-link"><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link ac:anchor="diagram_pane"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Draw on the diagram pane]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a relationship" /></ac:link></span></span> <span class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(51,102,255);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span></span><span class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(51,102,255);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span></span></span></span></li><li><ac:link ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[diagram pane]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><hr /><ol><li><span>In the </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Diagram palette]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, click the needed relationship.</li><li>Select the source shape of the path in the diagram pane.</li><li><p>Drop the path to the target shape.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9654161d-1979-4e30-ae00-9f595d56c4cf"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>When drawing a new path, the optimal route is chosen automatically (the path avoids other shapes).</li><li>When drawing a path between two elements, those shapes are bordered with a red or blue rectangle. The red highlight indicates that the path can not be drawn between these shapes. The blue highlight allows a path to be drawn. You can disable this highlighting in the <strong>Environment Options</strong> dialog under the Diagram node by setting the <strong>Show Manipulation Highlighting</strong> property value to <em>false</em>. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Customizing environment options" /></ac:link>.</li><li><p>To draw a path faster, use the <span class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Smart manipulator toolbar" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[smart manipulator toolbar]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span>.</p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1137a2db-b447-4db1-9f4f-f782c4e97275"><ac:parameter ac:name="">model_browser</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5aba8b2d-b39a-4243-a195-af680247d699">To create relationships between selected elements</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><hr /><ol><li>In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Model Browser" /></ac:link>, diagram pane, or table, right-click an element.</li><li>Choose<strong> Create Relation</strong> &gt;<strong> Outgoing </strong>/ <strong>Incoming</strong> and select the desired relationship type. The <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To </strong>/<strong> From</strong> dialog opens.</li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="011ad7d3-748c-4780-830d-013258e4ee2c">Do one or more of the following:</ac:inline-comment-marker><ul><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bd4d2faa-dbc1-4037-a06e-acb7a7a240ec">In the Model Browser, click the element to / from which you want to create a relationship.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li>On a diagram, click an element <span>to / from which you want to create a relationship.</span></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span><span>In the <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To</strong> /<strong> From</strong> dialog, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="21"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_elements_from_the_model.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a relationship" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>. In the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d630b714-c2d6-41af-ae2c-fa10eefba204">opened <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> /</span><strong> From </strong>dialog</ac:inline-comment-marker>, choose the element<span> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c91d2740-784a-4746-bb8a-438080c3138f">to / from</ac:inline-comment-marker> which you want to create a relationship.<br /></span></span></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1ee4b3db-2c93-480a-99ff-2ebeec2038a4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you do not know the exact location of the needed element, you can search for it in this step.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul></li><li>Click<strong> OK</strong>.</li></ol><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_new_relationship.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a relationship" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><p>The <strong>Active Selection</strong> area shows the elements currently <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="175a0936-7cef-4181-89da-4b1ef02a695b">selected <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">in the active diagram, table, matrix, map, or Model Browser</span></ac:inline-comment-marker>. It changes with each click of the different elements when you switch to another diagram.</p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7"><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7">To </ac:inline-comment-marker>choose one or several elements at a time<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7">, </ac:inline-comment-marker><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7">do one of the following</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><hr /><ul><li>Press Shift and click as many elements as you need.</li><li>Click and drag the mouse pointer to select as many elements as you need.</li></ul><p>The multiple selection works in one area at a time. For example, diagram pane, table, matrix, relation map, or Model Browser. If you need to select elements from several locations, you need to pin your current selection.</p><p>The <strong>Pinned Selection</strong> area shows the elements you choose to pin from the <strong>Active Selection</strong>. <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="71f937c8-7f88-4df0-8c70-b71778086cae">This is needed when you want to select elements from different locations (for example, you need to select elements from different diagrams, or from the diagram and Model Browser)</ac:inline-comment-marker>. In this case, you should pin each selection<span>.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8c813c97-cf66-48c5-8c29-03fa98a9c2c6"><ac:parameter ac:name="">specification</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create a relationship in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></p><hr /><ol><li>Open the element's Specification window and select the <strong>Relations</strong> property group.</li><li>Click the <strong>Create Outgoing</strong> button or <strong>Create Incoming</strong> button and then select the desired relation type from the list.</li><li>In the <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To/ From</strong> dialog select an element to/ from which you want to create a relation and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />The relation appears in the <strong>Relations</strong> property group.</li></ol><p>When the relationship is created, you can visually identify its direction (source and target elements) under the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Containment tab" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Containment tree]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> &gt;<strong> Relations</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="223ba6d2-d116-41cb-b0f8-2c839ac04069"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The arrow between source and target elements appears if the path is created using only directed relationships.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:title="Identifying the relationship's direction" ac:alt="Identifying the relationship's direction"><ri:attachment ri:filename="relation_direction_arrows_under_containment_tree_relations.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating a relationship" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714556 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00283: Creating a report including the Actor, Use Cases, and diagram image names

- page_id: `136714556`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714556/Creating+a+report+including+the+Actor+Use+Cases+and+diagram+image+names
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:41.125+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Linking report templates to the modeling tool > How modeling tool adds its elements to velocity context
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

135588550

135588551

135588549

**On this page**

3

135588551

#### Creating a report including the names of actors, use cases, and diagram images

This section will use part of the Inventory Control System.mdzip file as an example to illustrate how to reference to MagicDraw elements in a report. This example contains several packages. For instance, if you want to use the 'Use Case View' package, you need to know the elements inside the package before using it. To do this, follow the instructions in the previous section, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Your First Template' space=''].

The 'Use Case View' package contains three element types: Actor, UseCase, and Diagram that you can name in a template by typing **"$Actor**," **"$UseCase**," and **"$Diagram**" respectively. The following example shows you how to create a report that will print the names of the Actors, UseCases, and diagram images.

To create a report that includes the names of Actor, Use Cases, and diagram images

1. Open Microsoft Word or OpenOffice.org Writer.
2. Use the **#foreach** directive to iterate through **$Actor**, **$UseCase**, and **$Diagram**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Using #foreach directive to iterate through $Actor, $UseCase, and $Diagram in a Rich Text Format template.
3. Use Report Wizard to add the template to MagicDraw.
4. With the " Inventory Control System.mdzip " project open, open the Report Wizard dialog and select your template.
5. Select the element scope so it covers only the "Use Case View" package. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Selecting the Use Case View package from the model as the element scope to be included in the report.
6. Complete all steps in the **Report Wizard** dialog, and generate the report. The output will be shown as in the following figure. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Shows a partial report output. A full report will print all the names of the Actors, Use Cases, and the Use Case diagrams.

#### Glossary

- Actor An actor models a type of role played by an entity that interacts with the subjects of its associated use cases, e.g., by exchanging signal and data. Actors may represent roles played by human users, external hardware, or other systems.
- Element T he abstract root UML metaclass. It has no superclass in the hierarchy of UML elements. It is the superclass for all metaclasses in the UML infrastructure library. There is no general notation for an element. The specific subclasses of an element define their own notation. Each UMLelementhas acompositionrelationship to itself to support the capability for elements toownother elements.
- Use case A specification of behavior. A use case is a kind of BehavioredClassifier that represents a declaration of a set of offered Behaviors. Each use case specifies some behavior that a subject can perform in collaboration with one or more actors. A UseCase may be owned either by a Package or by a Classifier. Use cases are a means to capture the requirements of systems,( i.e. what systems are supposed to do).

#### Sample model

[ATTACHMENT filename='Inventory Control System.mdzip']250

#### More information about creating report templates

To learn more about working with report templates, visit the following pages:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Your First Template' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Linking report templates to the modeling tool' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exercise: Building a simple class diagram report' space='']
